summary: Gruber reviews the Apple Watch Series 7, arguing that its bigger display alone makes it a landmark design update, even if critics dismiss it as the only change. He traces the evolutionary design history of Apple Watch through three distinct eras: Series 0-3, Series 4-6, and now Series 7, noting how each generation refined the case shape and display. The slightly larger case (1mm) is meaningful at watch scale, and the redrawn watch faces subtly change the character of the device. He criticizes the new QWERTY keyboard as frustrating and too small, but praises the dark green aluminum finish. Gruber concludes that Apple has pursued a consistent Platonic ideal for Apple Watch from the start, and Series 7 brings it closer than ever.
key_points: The Apple Watch has evolved through three distinct design eras, with Series 4 and Series 7 marking the major transitions in case shape and display size
One millimeter is a meaningful difference at watch scale, even though it would be imperceptible on a laptop or tablet — the Series 7 cases are slightly bigger, not just the displays
The new QWERTY on-screen keyboard is frustrating to use and wastes space; voice dictation remains faster and more accurate
The green aluminum finish is much darker in person than Apple's product photography suggests, appearing nearly black in most lighting
Apple Watch should be evaluated as a watch you want to wear, not as a computer upgrade — buying one is about how it looks, not specs
money_quotes: Caring — really caring — about watches is a mixed blessing. It's a fun hobby. It's also a curse.
The affection a particular watch engenders is the result of all those details, regardless of whether the beholder notices any of them in particular.
Unlike any other product Apple has ever made, they really nailed the basic shape and look, the gestalt, on the first try. It was birthed as an iconic design.
Nothing new but a bigger display is enough to establish Series 7 as a landmark new design.
1 mm is a minor difference by the scale of most devices. But 1 mm is a meaningful difference at watch scale.
Apple Watch is a watch that happens to be a computer, not a computer that you happen to wear on your wrist.
Apple Watch has always known what it wanted to be, and Series 7 is closer to that than ever.
key_insight: Apple Watch Series 7 represents a minor but meaningful refinement toward a Platonic ideal that Apple has pursued since the original, with the bigger display and subtly larger case bringing it closer to what it was always meant to be.
